part.text:
Let me start by understanding the codebase and the current CI setup. The task is about making CI target support declarative, generating checked-in workflows from manifests, and enforcing that they stay in sync.

Let me explore the project structure first.
I'll explore the current CI setup and understand what needs to change.

part.text: Let me read the CI workflow and the mise.toml to understand the target support policy.

state.output:
<path>/app/mise.toml</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: # https://mise.jdx.dev
2: # Mise is the contributor entrypoint: it pins tools, installs hooks, and runs
3: # repository tasks.
4: 
5: experimental_monorepo_root = true
6: 
7: [settings]
8: lockfile = true
9: lockfile_platforms = ["linux-x64", "linux-arm64", "macos-arm64", "windows-x64"]
10: pin = true
11: experimental = true
12: python.uv_venv_auto = "create|source"
13: windows_default_file_shell_args = '"C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe" -e -c "source ""$MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT/.mise/bin/windows-msvc-path.sh""; exec ""C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe"" ""$@""" _'
14: windows_default_inline_shell_args = '"C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe" -e -c "source ""$MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT/.mise/bin/windows-msvc-path.sh""; eval ""$1""" _'
15: unix_default_file_shell_args = "bash"
16: unix_default_inline_shell_args = "bash -e -lc"
17: # On Windows, the Microsoft Store Python alias in WindowsApps can appear before
18: # mise-managed Python. Keep mise tool paths first so file tasks run pinned tools.
19: activate_aggressive = true
20: 
21: [monorepo]
22: config_roots = [".devcontainer", "bindings/*", "examples/*", "docs"]
23: 
24: [tools]
25: # General tooling
26: "aqua:jdx/usage" = "3.3.0" # CLI completion for mise tasks
27: "aqua:jdx/hk" = "1.45.0" # Linter orchestration
28: "aqua:apple/pkl" = "0.31.0" # hk config dependency
29: "aqua:dprint/dprint" = "0.53.2" # Formatter orchestration
30: "github:sargunv/jvl" = "2026.05.03" # JSON validator
31: "aqua:rhysd/actionlint" = "1.7.8" # GH Actions linter
32: "aqua:koalaman/shellcheck" = "0.11.0" # Shell script linter (used by actionlint)
33: 
34: # C/C++ ecosystem
35: "github:prefix-dev/pixi" = "0.68.1"
36: "aqua:Kitware/CMake" = "4.3.3"
37: "aqua:ninja-build/ninja" = "1.13.2"
38: "conda:clang-format" = "22.1.7"
39: "conda:clang-tools" = "22.1.7"
40: "conda:doxygen" = "1.13.2"
41: "conda:glslang" = "16.3.0"
42: "conda:pkg-config" = "0.29.2"
43: "conda:vulkan-tools" = "1.4.341.0"
44: "github:cmakefmt/cmakefmt" = "1.3.0"
45: "aqua:XcodesOrg/xcodes" = { version = "1.6.2", os = ["macos"] }
46: 
47: # Node.js ecosystem
48: "core:node" = "24.11.0"
49: "aqua:pnpm/pnpm" = "10.33.2"
50: 
51: # Java/Kotlin ecosystem
52: "core:java" = "temurin-25.0.3+9.0.LTS"
53: "aqua:facebook/ktfmt" = "0.62"
54: "github:google/google-java-format" = "1.35.0"
55: 
56: # Python ecosystem
57: "core:python" = "3.14.4"
58: "aqua:astral-sh/uv" = "0.11.11"
59: 
60: # Swift ecosystem
61: "core:swift" = { version = "6.3.1", os = ["macos"] }
62: "github:nicklockwood/SwiftFormat" = "0.61.1"
63: 
64: # Other ecosystems
65: "core:rust" = { version = "1.95.0", profile = "minimal", components = "rustfmt,clippy" }
66: "core:zig" = "0.16.0"
67: "core:go" = "1.26.3"
68: "core:dotnet" = "10.0.203"
69: 
70: [env]
71: UV_PYTHON = { value = "{{ tools.python.path }}", tools = true }
72: MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT = "{{config_root}}"
73: MLN_FFI_VARIANT = { required = true }
74: MLN_FFI_TARGET_TRIPLE = { required = true }
75: MLN_FFI_RENDER_BACKEND = { required = true }
76: MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR = { required = true }
77: MLN_FFI_CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE = "{{env.MLN_FFI_CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE | default(value=\"RelWithDebInfo\")}}"
78: MLN_FFI_INCLUDE_DIR = "{{config_root}}/include"
79: MLN_FFI_VULKAN_INCLUDE_DIR = "{{config_root}}/third_party/maplibre-native/vendor/Vulkan-Headers/include"
80: MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_INCLUDE_DIR = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/include"
81: MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/lib"
82: MLN_FFI_ZIG_TARGET = { required = true }
83: MLN_FFI_ZIG_BUILD_ARGS = { required = true }
84: MLN_FFI_TEST_TIMEOUT = "{{env.MLN_FFI_TEST_TIMEOUT | default(value=\"30s\")}}"
85: CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default"
86: PKG_CONFIG_PATH = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/lib/pkgconfig"
87: LIBCLANG_PATH = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/lib"
88: 
89: [hooks]
90: postinstall = [
91:   { task = "sync-submodules" },
92:   "hk install --mise --quiet",
93:   "pixi install --locked --quiet",
94:   # conda-forge ships libclang-13.dll, but clang-sys on Windows searches for libclang.dll.
95:   # https://github.com/KyleMayes/clang-sys/issues/213
96:   # https://github.com/conda-forge/llvmdev-feedstock/issues/376
97:   '{% if os() == "windows" %}libclang_bin="$(dirname "$MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR")/bin"; cp "$libclang_bin/libclang-13.dll" "$libclang_bin/libclang.dll"{% endif %}',
98:   "uv sync --locked --quiet",
99:   "pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --loglevel warn",
100:   "dotnet tool restore --tool-manifest dotnet-tools.json --verbosity quiet",
101:   "dotnet tool restore --tool-manifest bindings/dotnet/dotnet-tools.json --verbosity quiet",
102:   # Astro type stubs for vp typeCheck on docs sources (.astro/ is gitignored).
103:   "pnpm --filter docs exec astro sync",
104: ]
105: 
106: [tasks.configure]
107: depends = ["//:deps"]
108: run = [
109:   '''
110:   cmake_args=(
111:     -S .
112:     -B "$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR"
113:     -G Ninja
114:     -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE="$MLN_FFI_CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE"
115:     -DMLN_FFI_RENDER_BACKEND="$MLN_FFI_RENDER_BACKEND"
116:     -DMLN_FFI_OPENGL_CONTEXT_PROVIDER="$MLN_FFI_OPENGL_CONTEXT_PROVIDER"
117:     -DMLN_FFI_EGL_ROOT="$MLN_FFI_EGL_ROOT"
118:   )
119: 
120:   while IFS= read -r cmake_arg; do
121:     [[ -n "$cmake_arg" ]] && cmake_args+=("$cmake_arg")
122:   done <<< "${MLN_FFI_CMAKE_ARGS:-}"
123: 
124:   cmake "${cmake_args[@]}"
125:   ''',
126:   '''
127:   if [[ -f "$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR/compile_commands.json" ]]; then
128:     mkdir -p build/clangd
129:     compile_commands_link="build/clangd/compile_commands.json"
130:     compile_commands_tmp="${compile_commands_link}.tmp"
131:     rm -f "$compile_commands_tmp"
132:     ln -s "$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR/compile_commands.json" "$compile_commands_tmp"
133:     mv -f "$compile_commands_tmp" "$compile_commands_link"
134:   fi
135:   ''',
136: ]
137: 
138: [tasks.build]
139: depends = ["//:configure"]
140: run = '''
141: build_args=(
142:   --build "$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR"
143:   --parallel
144: )
145: 
146: cmake "${build_args[@]}"'''
147: 
148: [tasks.clean]
149: run = "rm -rf \"$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR\""
150: 
151: [tasks.deps]
152: description = "Install variant-specific managed dependencies."
153: run = '''
154: if [[ "$MLN_FFI_TARGET_TRIPLE" == "macos-arm64" ]]; then
155:   mise run deps:angle
156: fi
157: '''
158: 
159: [tasks.test]
160: depends = ["//:build"]
161: run = '''
162: (
163:   cd src/c_api/tests/zig
164:   zig build test {{ env.MLN_FFI_ZIG_BUILD_ARGS }} --test-timeout "$MLN_FFI_TEST_TIMEOUT" --summary all --verbose
165: )
166: (
167:   cd bindings/zig
168:   zig build test {{ env.MLN_FFI_ZIG_BUILD_ARGS }} --test-timeout "$MLN_FFI_TEST_TIMEOUT" --summary all --verbose
169: )
170: '''
171: 
172: [tasks.check]
173: run = "hk check --all"
174: 
175: [tasks.fix]
176: run = "hk fix --all"
177: 
178: [tasks.actions-up]
179: description = "Update and pin GitHub Actions steps."
180: run = "pnpm dlx actions-up"
181: 
182: [tasks._ktfmt]
183: description = "Called by dprint.jsonc"
184: hide = true
185: run = 'java -jar {{ tools["aqua:facebook/ktfmt"].path }}/ktfmt'
186: # ktfmt on windows doesn't work in git bash for some reason
187: shell = '{% if os() == "windows" %}cmd /c{% else %}sh -c -o errexit{% endif %}'
188: 
189: [tasks."ci:lint"]
190: run = [
191:   # Warm process-plugin cache before hk runs parallel dprint batches.
192:   "dprint output-resolved-config > /dev/null",
193:   "hk fix --all",
194:   "git update-index -q --refresh",
195:   # Lockfile drift is common across different mise builds
196:   "git diff --exit-code -- . ':(exclude)mise.lock'",
197:   "git diff --cached --exit-code -- . ':(exclude)mise.lock'",
198: ]
199: 
200: [tasks."ci:vulkan-info"]
201: description = "Print the active Vulkan loader and driver summary."
202: run = "vulkaninfo --summary"

(End of file - total 202 lines)
</content>

# https://mise.jdx.dev
# Mise is the contributor entrypoint: it pins tools, installs hooks, and runs
# repository tasks.

experimental_monorepo_root = true

[settings]
lockfile = true
lockfile_platforms = ["linux-x64", "linux-arm64", "macos-arm64", "windows-x64"]
pin = true
experimental = true
python.uv_venv_auto = "create|source"
windows_default_file_shell_args = '"C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe" -e -c "source ""$MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT/.mise/bin/windows-msvc-path.sh""; exec ""C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe"" ""$@""" _'
windows_default_inline_shell_args = '"C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe" -e -c "source ""$MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT/.mise/bin/windows-msvc-path.sh""; eval ""$1""" _'
unix_default_file_shell_args = "bash"
unix_default_inline_shell_args = "bash -e -lc"
# On Windows, the Microsoft Store Python alias in WindowsApps can appear before
# mise-managed Python. Keep mise tool paths first so file tasks run pinned tools.
activate_aggressive = true

display.text:
# https://mise.jdx.dev
# Mise is the contributor entrypoint: it pins tools, installs hooks, and runs
# repository tasks.

experimental_monorepo_root = true

[settings]
lockfile = true
lockfile_platforms = ["linux-x64", "linux-arm64", "macos-arm64", "windows-x64"]
pin = true
experimental = true
python.uv_venv_auto = "create|source"
windows_default_file_shell_args = '"C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe" -e -c "source ""$MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT/.mise/bin/windows-msvc-path.sh""; exec ""C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe"" ""$@""" _'
windows_default_inline_shell_args = '"C:/Program Files/Git/bin/bash.exe" -e -c "source ""$MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT/.mise/bin/windows-msvc-path.sh""; eval ""$1""" _'
unix_default_file_shell_args = "bash"
unix_default_inline_shell_args = "bash -e -lc"
# On Windows, the Microsoft Store Python alias in WindowsApps can appear before
# mise-managed Python. Keep mise tool paths first so file tasks run pinned tools.
activate_aggressive = true

[monorepo]
config_roots = [".devcontainer", "bindings/*", "examples/*", "docs"]

[tools]
# General tooling
"aqua:jdx/usage" = "3.3.0" # CLI completion for mise tasks
"aqua:jdx/hk" = "1.45.0" # Linter orchestration
"aqua:apple/pkl" = "0.31.0" # hk config dependency
"aqua:dprint/dprint" = "0.53.2" # Formatter orchestration
"github:sargunv/jvl" = "2026.05.03" # JSON validator
"aqua:rhysd/actionlint" = "1.7.8" # GH Actions linter
"aqua:koalaman/shellcheck" = "0.11.0" # Shell script linter (used by actionlint)

# C/C++ ecosystem
"github:prefix-dev/pixi" = "0.68.1"
"aqua:Kitware/CMake" = "4.3.3"
"aqua:ninja-build/ninja" = "1.13.2"
"conda:clang-format" = "22.1.7"
"conda:clang-tools" = "22.1.7"
"conda:doxygen" = "1.13.2"
"conda:glslang" = "16.3.0"
"conda:pkg-config" = "0.29.2"
"conda:vulkan-tools" = "1.4.341.0"
"github:cmakefmt/cmakefmt" = "1.3.0"
"aqua:XcodesOrg/xcodes" = { version = "1.6.2", os = ["macos"] }

# Node.js ecosystem
"core:node" = "24.11.0"
"aqua:pnpm/pnpm" = "10.33.2"

# Java/Kotlin ecosystem
"core:java" = "temurin-25.0.3+9.0.LTS"
"aqua:facebook/ktfmt" = "0.62"
"github:google/google-java-format" = "1.35.0"

# Python ecosystem
"core:python" = "3.14.4"
"aqua:astral-sh/uv" = "0.11.11"

# Swift ecosystem
"core:swift" = { version = "6.3.1", os = ["macos"] }
"github:nicklockwood/SwiftFormat" = "0.61.1"

# Other ecosystems
"core:rust" = { version = "1.95.0", profile = "minimal", components = "rustfmt,clippy" }
"core:zig" = "0.16.0"
"core:go" = "1.26.3"
"core:dotnet" = "10.0.203"

[env]
UV_PYTHON = { value = "{{ tools.python.path }}", tools = true }
MLN_FFI_REPO_ROOT = "{{config_root}}"
MLN_FFI_VARIANT = { required = true }
MLN_FFI_TARGET_TRIPLE = { required = true }
MLN_FFI_RENDER_BACKEND = { required = true }
MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR = { required = true }
MLN_FFI_CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE = "{{env.MLN_FFI_CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE | default(value=\"RelWithDebInfo\")}}"
MLN_FFI_INCLUDE_DIR = "{{config_root}}/include"
MLN_FFI_VULKAN_INCLUDE_DIR = "{{config_root}}/third_party/maplibre-native/vendor/Vulkan-Headers/include"
MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_INCLUDE_DIR = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/include"
MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/lib"
MLN_FFI_ZIG_TARGET = { required = true }
MLN_FFI_ZIG_BUILD_ARGS = { required = true }
MLN_FFI_TEST_TIMEOUT = "{{env.MLN_FFI_TEST_TIMEOUT | default(value=\"30s\")}}"
CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default"
PKG_CONFIG_PATH = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/lib/pkgconfig"
LIBCLANG_PATH = "{{config_root}}/.pixi/envs/default/lib"

[hooks]
postinstall = [
  { task = "sync-submodules" },
  "hk install --mise --quiet",
  "pixi install --locked --quiet",
  # conda-forge ships libclang-13.dll, but clang-sys on Windows searches for libclang.dll.
  # https://github.com/KyleMayes/clang-sys/issues/213
  # https://github.com/conda-forge/llvmdev-feedstock/issues/376
  '{% if os() == "windows" %}libclang_bin="$(dirname "$MLN_FFI_DEPENDENCY_LIBRARY_DIR")/bin"; cp "$libclang_bin/libclang-13.dll" "$libclang_bin/libclang.dll"{% endif %}',
  "uv sync --locked --quiet",
  "pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --loglevel warn",
  "dotnet tool restore --tool-manifest dotnet-tools.json --verbosity quiet",
  "dotnet tool restore --tool-manifest bindings/dotnet/dotnet-tools.json --verbosity quiet",
  # Astro type stubs for vp typeCheck on docs sources (.astro/ is gitignored).
  "pnpm --filter docs exec astro sync",
]

[tasks.configure]
depends = ["//:deps"]
run = [
  '''
  cmake_args=(
    -S .
    -B "$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR"
    -G Ninja
    -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE="$MLN_FFI_CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE"
    -DMLN_FFI_RENDER_BACKEND="$MLN_FFI_RENDER_BACKEND"
    -DMLN_FFI_OPENGL_CONTEXT_PROVIDER="$MLN_FFI_OPENGL_CONTEXT_PROVIDER"
    -DMLN_FFI_EGL_ROOT="$MLN_FFI_EGL_ROOT"
  )

  while IFS= read -r cmake_arg; do
    [[ -n "$cmake_arg" ]] && cmake_args+=("$cmake_arg")
  done <<< "${MLN_FFI_CMAKE_ARGS:-}"

  cmake "${cmake_args[@]}"
  ''',
  '''
  if [[ -f "$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR/compile_commands.json" ]]; then
    mkdir -p build/clangd
    compile_commands_link="build/clangd/compile_commands.json"
    compile_commands_tmp="${compile_commands_link}.tmp"
    rm -f "$compile_commands_tmp"
    ln -s "$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR/compile_commands.json" "$compile_commands_tmp"
    mv -f "$compile_commands_tmp" "$compile_commands_link"
  fi
  ''',
]

[tasks.build]
depends = ["//:configure"]
run = '''
build_args=(
  --build "$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR"
  --parallel
)

cmake "${build_args[@]}"'''

[tasks.clean]
run = "rm -rf \"$MLN_FFI_BUILD_DIR\""

[tasks.deps]
description = "Install variant-specific managed dependencies."
run = '''
if [[ "$MLN_FFI_TARGET_TRIPLE" == "macos-arm64" ]]; then
  mise run deps:angle
fi
'''

[tasks.test]
depends = ["//:build"]
run = '''
(
  cd src/c_api/tests/zig
  zig build test {{ env.MLN_FFI_ZIG_BUILD_ARGS }} --test-timeout "$MLN_FFI_TEST_TIMEOUT" --summary all --verbose
)
(
  cd bindings/zig
  zig build test {{ env.MLN_FFI_ZIG_BUILD_ARGS }} --test-timeout "$MLN_FFI_TEST_TIMEOUT" --summary all --verbose
)
'''

[tasks.check]
run = "hk check --all"

[tasks.fix]
run = "hk fix --all"

[tasks.actions-up]
description = "Update and pin GitHub Actions steps."
run = "pnpm dlx actions-up"

[tasks._ktfmt]
description = "Called by dprint.jsonc"
hide = true
run = 'java -jar {{ tools["aqua:facebook/ktfmt"].path }}/ktfmt'
# ktfmt on windows doesn't work in git bash for some reason
shell = '{% if os() == "windows" %}cmd /c{% else %}sh -c -o errexit{% endif %}'

[tasks."ci:lint"]
run = [
  # Warm process-plugin cache before hk runs parallel dprint batches.
  "dprint output-resolved-config > /dev/null",
  "hk fix --all",
  "git update-index -q --refresh",
  # Lockfile drift is common across different mise builds
  "git diff --exit-code -- . ':(exclude)mise.lock'",
  "git diff --cached --exit-code -- . ':(exclude)mise.lock'",
]

[tasks."ci:vulkan-info"]
description = "Print the active Vulkan loader and driver summary."
run = "vulkaninfo --summary"

state.title: mise.toml

name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

permissions:
  contents: read

concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.ref }}

  hygiene:

    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 30

display.text:
name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

permissions:
  contents: read

concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' }}

jobs:
  hygiene:
    name: hygiene
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 30
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-ci-deps
      - run: mise run ci:lint

  docs:
    name: docs
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 60
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-ci-deps
      - run: mise run //docs:build

  variants:
    name: variant / ${{ matrix.mise_env }}
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.runner }}
    timeout-minutes: 120
    env:
      MISE_ENV: ${{ matrix.mise_env }}
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          - mise_env: linux-arm64-egl
            runner: ubuntu-24.04-arm
          - mise_env: linux-arm64-vulkan
            runner: ubuntu-24.04-arm
          - mise_env: linux-x64-egl
            runner: ubuntu-latest
          - mise_env: linux-x64-vulkan
            runner: ubuntu-latest
          - mise_env: macos-arm64-metal
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: macos-arm64-vulkan
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: macos-arm64-egl
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: ios-arm64-metal
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: ios-simulator-arm64-metal
            runner: macos-latest
          - mise_env: windows-x64-vulkan
            runner: windows-2022
          - mise_env: windows-x64-wgl
            runner: windows-2022
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-ci-deps

      - run: mise run configure
      - name: Build native library
        if: ${{ startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run build
      - name: Test native library
        if: ${{ !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run test

      - name: Build Java FFM binding
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/java-ffm:build
      - name: Build Java JNI binding
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/java-jni:build
      - name: Test .NET binding
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/dotnet:test
      # linux-arm64: no Kotlin/Native host — https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/KT-36871
      # windows: no MSVC target — https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/SKIKO-446
      - name: Test Kotlin/Native binding
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'windows-') && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'linux-arm64-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/kotlin-native:build
      - name: Test Rust binding
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/rust:ci
      - name: Test Swift binding
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'macos-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/swift:test
      - name: Test Zig binding
        if: ${{ !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //bindings/zig:ci

      - name: Build LWJGL example
        if: ${{ contains(matrix.mise_env, 'vulkan') }}
        run: mise run //examples/lwjgl-map:build
      - name: Test Rust example
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //examples/rust-map:ci
      - name: Build Swift example
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env == 'macos-arm64-metal' }}
        run: mise run //examples/swift-map:build
      - name: Build Zig example
        if: ${{ matrix.mise_env != 'macos-arm64-egl' && !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //examples/zig-map:build
      - name: Run Zig readback example
        if: ${{ !startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-') }}
        run: mise run //examples/zig-readback:run

  required:
    name: ci-required
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 5
    if: ${{ always() }}
    needs:
      - hygiene
      - docs
      - variants
    steps:
      - name: Fail if required jobs failed
        if: ${{ contains(needs.*.result, 'failure') || contains(needs.*.result, 'cancelled') || contains(needs.*.result, 'skipped') }}
        run: |
          echo '${{ toJSON(needs) }}'
          exit 1

      - run: true

state.title: .github/workflows/ci.yml

part.text:
Now I see the CI workflow. The `variants` matrix defines the target support policy. The `mise_env` values map to runners. Let me look at the `.mise` directory and the mise envs to understand how variants are defined.

The key insight is that the CI matrix in ci.yml duplicates the list of supported variants. There are mise envs like `linux-x64-vulkan`, etc. mentioned in AGENTS.md. Let me find where those envs are defined.

Let me explore the `.mise` directory and look for env configs.
Let me explore the mise configuration structure to understand where the variants are defined.

input.command: ls -la /app/.mise/ && echo "---" && ls -la /app/.devcontainer/ && echo "---MISERC---" && cat /app/.miserc.toml

<path>/app/pixi.toml</path>

<content>
1: # https://pixi.prefix.dev/
2: # Pixi installs the desktop native libraries used by local builds.
3: 
4: [workspace]
5: channels = ["conda-forge"]
6: platforms = ["linux-64", "linux-aarch64", "osx-64", "osx-arm64", "win-64"]
7: 
8: [system-requirements]
9: macos = "14.0"
10: 
11: [dependencies]
12: # Native support library used by binding generators.
13: libclang = ">=19,<20"
14: 
15: # Native libraries linked by project code, examples, and vendored MapLibre Native.
16: icu = ">=78,<79"
17: libcurl = ">=8,<8.20"
18: libjpeg-turbo = ">=3.1,<4"
19: libpng = ">=1.6,<2"
20: libuv = ">=1,<2"
21: libwebp = ">=1.6,<2"

# https://pixi.prefix.dev/
# Pixi installs the desktop native libraries used by local builds.

[workspace]
channels = ["conda-forge"]
platforms = ["linux-64", "linux-aarch64", "osx-64", "osx-arm64", "win-64"]

[system-requirements]
macos = "14.0"

[dependencies]
# Native support library used by binding generators.
libclang = ">=19,<20"

# Native libraries linked by project code, examples, and vendored MapLibre Native.
icu = ">=78,<79"
libcurl = ">=8,<8.20"
libjpeg-turbo = ">=3.1,<4"
libpng = ">=1.6,<2"
libuv = ">=1,<2"
libwebp = ">=1.6,<2"
sdl3 = ">=3.4,<4"
libvulkan-loader = ">=1.4,<2"
zlib = ">=1.3,<2"

[target.linux-64.dependencies]
libxcrypt1 = ">=4.4,<5"
libegl-devel = ">=1.7.0,<2"
libopengl-devel = ">=1.7.0,<2"
libgles-devel = ">=1.7.0,<2"

[target.linux-aarch64.dependencies]
libxcrypt1 = ">=4.4,<5"
libegl-devel = ">=1.7.0,<2"
libopengl-devel = ">=1.7.0,<2"
libgles-devel = ">=1.7.0,<2"

[target.osx-arm64.dependencies]
moltenvk = ">=1.4.1,<2"

[target.win-64.dependencies]
dlfcn-win32 = ">=1.4,<2"

part.text:
Now I understand the structure. The `.mise/config.*.toml` files define the variants. The CI workflow `.github/workflows/ci.yml` has a matrix that lists all the variants and maps them to runners. This is duplicated policy.

Let me look at a few config files to understand the structure.

state.title: .mise/config.windows-x64-wgl.toml

part.text:
Now I have a good understanding. The `.mise/config.*.toml` files are the manifests of supported variants. The CI workflow's matrix duplicates this list and maps each to a runner. The runner mapping is NOT in the config files—it's only in the CI workflow.

So the policy that's "trapped in a hand-written workflow" is:
1. The list of variants to test (duplicates the set of `.mise/config.*.toml` files)
2. The runner mapping per variant
3. The per-binding/example test conditions (which variants run which binding tests)

Let me understand the conditions more:
- `macos-arm64-egl` is excluded from most binding/example tests
- `ios-*` is excluded from binding/example tests (build only)
- `windows-*` excluded from Kotlin/Native
- `linux-arm64-*` excluded from Kotlin/Native
- LWJGL only on vulkan variants
- Swift binding/example only on macos-*
- Swift example only on macos-arm64-metal

These conditions encode real policy about what's supported where. Some of this is genuinely complex CI policy, not just "which targets we support."

The task says: "Make it declarative, generate the checked-in workflow from manifests, and enforce that it stays in sync. Preserve current CI behavior unless it is clearly duplicated policy."

The clearest duplicated policy is the **list of variants and their runners** in the matrix, which must match the `.mise/config.*.toml` files. That's the core drift problem: when you add a new variant config file, you have to remember to add it to the CI matrix.

The per-binding conditions are arguably more about "what's supported per binding" — these could also be encoded in manifests, but that's a bigger scope. Let me focus on the core: the variant matrix and runner mapping.

Let me think about what the cleanest approach is. I'll need to:
1. Create a manifest that declares the CI targets (variants + runners + per-job inclusion policy)
2. Write a generator that produces the matrix section of the workflow (or the whole workflow)
3. Add a check to enforce the generated workflow stays in sync
4. Test the generator

Let me check if there are existing scripts and how mise tasks are structured, and whether there's an existing tooling convention (Python with uv, or a mise task).

Let me look at the scripts directory and .mise/tasks.
Let me look at the remaining variant configs, scripts directory, and existing tasks to understand conventions.

---SETUP ACTION---
name: Setup CI deps
description: Install sccache and mise toolchain

runs:
  using: composite
  steps:
    - name: Enable Git long paths
      if: ${{ runner.os == 'Windows' }}
      shell: pwsh
      run: git config --global core.longpaths true

    - name: Cache MapLibre Native source
      uses: actions/cache@27d5ce7f107fe9357f9df03efb73ab90386fccae # v5.0.5
      with:
        path: |
          third_party/maplibre-native
          .git/modules/third_party/maplibre-native
        key: mln-src-v2-${{ hashFiles('.gitmodules', 'cmake/mln_version.cmake') }}

    - name: Install sccache
      uses: mozilla-actions/sccache-action@9e7fa8a12102821edf02ca5dbea1acd0f89a2696 # v0.0.10

    - name: Use Linux software graphics drivers
      if: ${{ runner.os == 'Linux' }}
      shell: bash
      run: |
        sudo apt-get update
        sudo apt-get install --no-install-recommends --yes \
          libegl-mesa0 \
          libgl1-mesa-dri \
          libvulkan1 \
          mesa-utils \
          mesa-utils-extra \
          mesa-vulkan-drivers
        {
          echo "EGL_PLATFORM=surfaceless"
          echo "LIBGL_ALWAYS_SOFTWARE=true"
        } >> "$GITHUB_ENV"

    - name: Use Windows software graphics drivers
      if: ${{ runner.os == 'Windows' }}
      shell: pwsh
      run: |
        $mesaVersion = "26.0.8"
        $mesaArchive = Join-Path $env:RUNNER_TEMP "mesa3d.7z"
        $mesaRoot = Join-Path $env:RUNNER_TEMP "mesa3d"
        $mesaUrl = "https://github.com/pal1000/mesa-dist-win/releases/download/$mesaVersion/mesa3d-$mesaVersion-release-msvc.7z"

        Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $mesaUrl -OutFile $mesaArchive
        $expectedHash = "A438C26C2752726916E455F9AD121F8A7E3CFECF8626251ABF8E5B3E129D8497"
        $actualHash = (Get-FileHash $mesaArchive -Algorithm SHA256).Hash
        if ($actualHash -ne $expectedHash) {
          throw "Mesa archive hash mismatch: expected $expectedHash, got $actualHash"
        }
        7z x $mesaArchive "-o$mesaRoot" -y | Out-Null

        $mesaWglDriver = Get-ChildItem $mesaRoot -Recurse -Filter "libgallium_wgl.dll" |
          Where-Object { $_.FullName -match "x64" } |
          Select-Object -First 1
        if (-not $mesaWglDriver) {
          throw "Missing Mesa WGL driver in $mesaArchive"
        }

        $driverManifest = Get-ChildItem $mesaRoot -Recurse -Filter "lvp_icd.x86_64.json" |
          Select-Object -First 1
        if (-not $driverManifest) {
          throw "Missing lavapipe Vulkan ICD manifest in $mesaArchive"
        }

        $mesaBin = $mesaWglDriver.Directory.FullName
        $mesaWglSystemDriver = Join-Path $env:WINDIR "System32\mesadrv.dll"
        Copy-Item $mesaWglDriver.FullName $mesaWglSystemDriver -Force

        $openglDriverKey = "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\OpenGLDrivers\MSOGL"
        New-Item -Path $openglDriverKey -Force | Out-Null
        New-ItemProperty -Path $openglDriverKey -Name DLL -PropertyType String -Value "mesadrv.dll" -Force | Out-Null
        New-ItemProperty -Path $openglDriverKey -Name DriverVersion -PropertyType DWord -Value 1 -Force | Out-Null
        New-ItemProperty -Path $openglDriverKey -Name Flags -PropertyType DWord -Value 1 -Force | Out-Null
        New-ItemProperty -Path $openglDriverKey -Name Version -PropertyType DWord -Value 2 -Force | Out-Null

        $driverKey = "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Khronos\Vulkan\Drivers"
        New-Item -Path $driverKey -Force | Out-Null
        New-ItemProperty -Path $driverKey -Name $driverManifest.FullName -PropertyType DWord -Value 0 -Force | Out-Null

        "GALLIUM_DRIVER=llvmpipe" >> $env:GITHUB_ENV
        "Path=$mesaBin;$env:Path" >> $env:GITHUB_ENV

    - name: Cache Cargo packages
      uses: actions/cache@27d5ce7f107fe9357f9df03efb73ab90386fccae # v5.0.5
      with:
        path: |
          ~/.cargo/registry/index/
          ~/.cargo/registry/cache/
          ~/.cargo/git/db/
        key: ${{ runner.os }}-${{ runner.arch }}-cargo-registry-v1-${{ hashFiles('**/Cargo.lock') }}

    - name: Cache Gradle
      uses: actions/cache@27d5ce7f107fe9357f9df03efb73ab90386fccae # v5.0.5
      with:
        path: |
          ~/.gradle/caches
          ~/.gradle/wrapper
        key: ${{ runner.os }}-${{ runner.arch }}-gradle-${{ hashFiles('**/*.gradle*', '**/gradle-wrapper.properties') }}
        restore-keys: |
          ${{ runner.os }}-${{ runner.arch }}-gradle-

    - name: Trim Windows PATH before mise activation
      if: ${{ runner.os == 'Windows' }}
      shell: pwsh
      run: |
        $workspaceDrive = Split-Path -Qualifier $env:GITHUB_WORKSPACE
        # Git Bash maps /tmp to the checkout drive; create it before mise hooks run.
        New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path (Join-Path $workspaceDrive "tmp") | Out-Null

        $paths = @(
          if ($env:SCCACHE_PATH) { Split-Path -Parent $env:SCCACHE_PATH }
          if ($env:Path) { $env:Path -split ';' }
          "C:\Program Files\Git\bin",
          "C:\Program Files\Git\usr\bin",
          "$env:SystemRoot\System32",
          "$env:SystemRoot",
          "$env:SystemRoot\System32\Wbem",
          "$env:SystemRoot\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0",
          "C:\Program Files\PowerShell\7",
          "C:\Program Files\Git\cmd"
        ) | Where-Object { $_ -and (Test-Path $_) }

        "Path=$($paths -join ';')" >> $env:GITHUB_ENV

        $gradleTemp = $env:RUNNER_TEMP -replace '\\', '/'
        $gradleOptions = "-Djava.io.tmpdir=$gradleTemp"
        if ($env:GRADLE_OPTS) {
          $gradleOptions = "$gradleOptions $env:GRADLE_OPTS"
        }
        "GRADLE_OPTS=$gradleOptions" >> $env:GITHUB_ENV

    - name: Install mise toolchain
      uses: jdx/mise-action@1648a7812b9aeae629881980618f079932869151 # v4.0.1
      with:
        cache: true
        cache_key_prefix: mise-v2
        install: true

    - name: Wire sccache into CMake
      shell: bash
      run: |
        {
          echo "SCCACHE_GHA_ENABLED=true"
          echo "SCCACHE_GHA_VERSION=${MLN_FFI_TARGET_TRIPLE}"
          echo "SCCACHE_BASEDIRS=${GITHUB_WORKSPACE}"
          echo "CMAKE_C_COMPILER_LAUNCHER=sccache"
          echo "CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER_LAUNCHER=sccache"
        } >> "$GITHUB_ENV"

    - name: Diagnose EGL setup
      if: ${{ runner.os == 'Linux' }}
      shell: bash
      run: |
        mise exec -- bash -euo pipefail -c '
          env | sort | grep -E "^(EGL|LIBGL|MESA|LD_LIBRARY_PATH|__EGL|MLN_FFI|VK_)" || true
          command -v eglinfo
          EGL_LOG_LEVEL=debug LIBGL_DEBUG=verbose LD_DEBUG=libs eglinfo || true
        '

    - name: Diagnose Vulkan setup
      if: ${{ contains(env.MISE_ENV, 'vulkan') }}
      shell: bash
      run: mise run ci:vulkan-info

    # The mise action may restore Rust metadata without all rustup-managed
    # component files. Repair the toolchain only when rustup cannot add the
    # configured components or the component binaries still cannot run.
    - name: Ensure Rust components
      shell: bash
      run: |
        ensure_rust_components() {
          mise exec -- rustup component add rustfmt clippy &&
            mise exec -- cargo fmt --version &&
            mise exec -- cargo clippy --version
        }

        if ! ensure_rust_components; then
          mise install --force rust
          ensure_rust_components
        fi

part.text:
Now I have a complete picture. Let me analyze the CI workflow's "variants" matrix carefully:

The matrix `include` has 11 variants:
1. linux-arm64-egl → ubuntu-24.04-arm
2. linux-arm64-vulkan → ubuntu-24.04-arm
3. linux-x64-egl → ubuntu-latest
4. linux-x64-vulkan → ubuntu-latest
5. macos-arm64-metal → macos-latest
6. macos-arm64-vulkan → macos-latest
7. macos-arm64-egl → macos-latest
8. ios-arm64-metal → macos-latest
9. ios-simulator-arm64-metal → macos-latest
10. windows-x64-vulkan → windows-2022
11. windows-x64-wgl → windows-2022

These match exactly the 11 `.mise/config.*.toml` files. Good.

The runner mapping per variant:
- linux-arm64-* → ubuntu-24.04-arm
- linux-x64-* → ubuntu-latest
- macos-arm64-* / ios-* → macos-latest
- windows-x64-* → windows-2022

Then there are the per-job conditions. Let me enumerate the conditions and understand them as policies:

1. `Build native library`: if `startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-')`
2. `Test native library`: if `!startsWith(matrix.mise_env, 'ios-')`
3. `Build Java FFM binding`: if `!= macos-arm64-egl && !ios-`
4. `Build Java JNI binding`: if `!= macos-arm64-egl && !ios-`
5. `Test .NET binding`: if `!= macos-arm64-egl && !ios-`
6. `Test Kotlin/Native binding`: if `!= macos-arm64-egl && !ios- && !windows- && !linux-arm64-`
7. `Test Rust binding`: if `!= macos-arm64-egl && !ios-`
8. `Test Swift binding`: if `!= macos-arm64-egl && startsWith macos-`
9. `Test Zig binding`: if `!ios-`
10. `Build LWJGL example`: if `contains vulkan`
11. `Test Rust example`: if `!= macos-arm64-egl && !ios-`
12. `Build Swift example`: if `== macos-arm64-metal`
13. `Build Zig example`: if `!= macos-arm64-egl && !ios-`
14. `Run Zig readback example`: if `!ios-`

So the policies are:
- `macos-arm64-egl`: a special variant that doesn't run most bindings/examples. This is a variant-level "binding-capable" flag.
- `ios-*`: build-only, no bindings/examples.
- `windows-*`, `linux-arm64-*`: no Kotlin/Native.
- LWJGL: vulkan only.
- Swift: macos only.

Now, the task says: "Make it declarative, generate the checked-in workflow from manifests, and enforce that it stays in sync. Preserve current CI behavior unless it is clearly duplicated policy."

The clearly duplicated policy is the **list of variants** (matrix entries) which must match the set of `.mise/config.*.toml` files. That's the core drift problem. The runner mapping is also derived policy (can be derived from variant name prefix).

Now the question of scope: should I encode ALL the per-binding conditions into the manifest, or just the variant list + runner mapping?

The task says "Make it declarative" and "generate the checked-in workflow from manifests." The most robust solution that addresses the drift problem is to make the variant list a manifest, generate the matrix, and validate. The per-binding conditions could be considered "CI behavior" that's harder to make fully declarative.

However, "Preserve current CI behavior unless it is clearly duplicated policy." The clearest duplicated policy is the variant list. Let me think about what the best architecture is.

I think the cleanest approach that solves the stated problem well:

**Option A: Generate just the matrix from a manifest.** Keep the rest of the workflow hand-written. Add a validation check that the matrix matches the manifest + the set of config files.

**Option B: Generate the entire variants job (matrix + steps with conditions) from a manifest.** This is more fully declarative but more complex.

**Option C: Generate the entire workflow file from a manifest.**

The task says "generate the checked-in workflow from manifests." That suggests generating the workflow (or at least the variants job) from a manifest. But it also says "Preserve current CI behavior unless it is clearly duplicated policy" and "Keep normal contributor workflow simple."

I think the right balance: Generate the `variants` job's matrix and the per-step conditions from a single manifest that declares, per variant, the runner and which "capabilities"/test-jobs it supports. This makes the variant→runner and variant→jobs mapping declarative and the generator produces the whole `variants` job. The hygiene/docs/required jobs stay as-is (not duplicated policy).

Actually, let me reconsider. The per-step conditions in the workflow encode real, nuanced policy (e.g., "no Kotlin/Native on linux-arm64 because no host compiler"). Encoding these as per-variant boolean flags in a manifest is exactly the kind of declarative policy that prevents drift and is testable. So I should encode them.

But wait — there's a subtlety. The conditions are about job inclusion. If I make each variant declare which jobs it runs, then adding a new binding test step requires editing the generator. That's fine.

Let me think about the cleanest design that:
1. Solves the drift (variant list ↔ config files)
2. Is declarative
3. Is testable (generator has tests)
4. Keeps contributor workflow simple (run a mise task to regenerate; a check enforces sync)
5. Preserves current behavior

I'll design a manifest in TOML (consistent with the repo's mise/pixi TOML style) that declares:
- The set of CI targets. Each target has:
  - `variant` (the mise env name, must match a `.mise/config.<variant>.toml`)
  - `runner` (GitHub Actions runner label)
- A set of "jobs" (the per-variant steps), each with:
  - name, run command, and a `where` predicate OR an explicit `variants` allow-list.

Actually, the conditions are expressed as predicates on variant names. Let me look at them as predicates:
- `ios-` prefix
- `!= macos-arm64-egl`
- `contains vulkan`
- `startsWith macos-`
- `== macos-arm64-metal`
- `!windows-`, `!linux-arm64-`

I think the cleanest and most explicit/declarative approach is: each variant explicitly lists the jobs it runs. This avoids fragile predicate string-matching and is fully declarative. But that's verbose (11 variants × ~14 jobs).

Alternatively, encode jobs with a `where` expression language. That's more compact but introduces a mini-DSL.

Hmm. Let me reconsider what "manifest" means here. I think the cleanest is:

A single manifest file declaring:
- targets: list of {variant, runner}
- The mapping is validated against config files.

And for the jobs, I think we keep them in the generator/template but driven by the manifest's declared capabilities. Actually, the conditions reference properties that can be derived from the variant: os family (linux/macos/ios/windows), arch (arm64/x64), render backend (vulkan/metal/opengl/egl), and a "special" macos-arm64-egl marker.

Let me reconsider scope. The user said this is for "CI target support policy." The core is which targets we support and where they run. The per-binding conditions are arguably "binding support policy" which is related but a different concern.

Given the explicit instruction "Preserve current CI behavior unless it is clearly duplicated policy," I should be careful not to over-engineer. The most clearly duplicated policy is the **variant list** (it must match the config files). The runner mapping is derivable.

I think the best approach that's declarative, generates a workflow, enforces sync, and is testable:

**Manifest** declares the CI matrix: each target variant + its runner. This is the single source of truth for "which targets CI supports and where."

**Generator** reads the manifest and produces the `variants` job's `matrix.include`. To preserve the per-step conditions, the generator needs to know them too. So I have two sub-options:

(a) The manifest only has targets+runners; the generator injects the matrix into a template that still has hand-written step conditions. The generator must keep the matrix in sync. Validation: manifest targets == set of config files == matrix in ci.yml.

(b) The manifest has targets+runners AND per-target job capabilities; the generator emits the whole variants job with conditions.

I lean toward (b) being more fully "declarative" and addressing "keeps drifting" better, because the per-binding conditions also drift. But it's a bigger change.

Actually, let me reconsider. The phrase "CI target support policy" strongly points at the targets themselves (the matrix). The drift problem ("keeps drifting") is most naturally about the variant list getting out of sync with the config files. I'll ask the user about scope since the instructions explicitly permit `ask_user` for "consequential architecture or scope questions."

Let me decide what to ask. The key scope question: Should the manifest/generator cover just the variant matrix (targets + runners), or also the per-binding/per-example step conditions?

Let me also consider: what language/tool for the generator? The repo uses mise tasks heavily. There's Python (uv) available, Go, Rust, Zig, etc. A Python script run via mise is natural and easy to test. The repo has `pyproject.toml` and uv. Let me check pyproject.toml.

1: // hk orchestrates checks for pre-commit, `mise run check`, and `mise run fix`.
2: // Tools used here must be installed by `mise install`.
3: 
4: amends "package://github.com/jdx/hk/releases/download/v1.45.0/hk@1.45.0#/Config.pkl"
5: 
6: import "package://github.com/jdx/hk/releases/download/v1.45.0/hk@1.45.0#/Builtins.pkl"
7: 
8: local lintSteps = new Mapping<String, Step> {
9:   ["dprint"] = (Builtins.dprint) {
10:     // the default batch=false hits the cmd length limit on windows
11:     batch = true
12:   }
13:   ["actionlint"] {
14:     glob = List(".github/workflows/*.yml", ".github/workflows/*.yaml")
15:     check = "actionlint {{files}}"
16:   }
17:   ["jvl"] {
18:     glob = List("**/*.json", "**/*.jsonc")
19:     check = "jvl check {{files}}"
20:   }
21:   // Mirrors Builtins.`vp-check` (available in hk 1.46+). dprint owns formatting.
22:   ["vp-check"] {
23:     glob = List(
24:       "**/*.js",
25:       "**/*.mjs",
26:       "**/*.cjs",
27:       "**/*.ts",
28:       "**/*.mts",
29:       "**/*.cts",
30:       "**/*.jsx",
31:       "**/*.tsx",
32:       "**/*.vue",
33:       "**/*.svelte",
34:       "**/*.astro",
35:     )
36:     exclude = List("third_party/**")
37:     check = "pnpm exec vp check --no-fmt {{files}}"
38:     fix = "pnpm exec vp check --no-fmt --fix {{files}}"
39:   }
40:   ["ruff"] {
41:     glob = List("**/*.py")
42:     exclude = List("third_party/**")
43:     check = "uv run ruff check {{files}} && uv run ruff format --check {{files}}"
44:     fix = "uv run ruff check --fix {{files}} && uv run ruff format {{files}}"
45:   }
46:   ["ty"] {
47:     glob = List("**/*.py")
48:     exclude = List("third_party/**")
49:     check = "uv run ty check --error-on-warning .mise"
50:   }
51: }
52: 
53: hooks {
54:   ["pre-commit"] {
55:     fix = true
56:     stash = "git"
57:     steps {
58:       ...lintSteps
59:     }
60:   }
61:   ["check"] {
62:     steps {
63:       ...lintSteps
64:     }
65:   }
66:   ["fix"] {
67:     fix = true
68:     steps {
69:       ...lintSteps
70:     }
71:   }
72: }

(End of file - total 72 lines)
</content>

part.text:
Now I have a good understanding of the conventions:
- Python via uv for tooling (ruff, ty). The `.mise` dir has Python type checking (`ty check --error-on-warning .mise`).
- hk orchestrates lint, including actionlint on `.github/workflows/*.yml`.
- TOML is the format for manifests.
- The generator could be a Python script with tests.

Now, the key architecture decision. Let me think carefully about what to ask the user.

The clearly duplicated policy is the **variant list** (matrix ↔ `.mise/config.*.toml`). The runner mapping is derivable. The per-step conditions encode binding/example support policy.

I think there are two reasonable scopes:
1. **Matrix-only**: manifest declares targets (variant+runner), generator writes the matrix into ci.yml, validation ensures manifest == config files == workflow matrix. The per-step conditions stay hand-written in the workflow. Smaller, lower risk, but the per-binding conditions can still drift.

2. **Full variants job**: manifest declares targets + which jobs each runs; generator emits the entire `variants` job (matrix + all steps with conditions). Fully declarative for the variants job; larger change.

The task strongly hints at scope 2 ("generate the checked-in workflow from manifests"), but scope 1 also satisfies "declarative + generate + enforce sync." Given the explicit invitation to ask about "consequential architecture or scope questions," and that this is a real fork in the road affecting how much CI behavior I touch, I should ask one focused scope question.

But I want to avoid asking if the answer is reasonably clear. Let me reconsider. The phrase "CI target support policy is trapped in a hand-written workflow and keeps drifting" — "target support policy" = which targets are supported. That's the matrix. The per-binding conditions are about binding support, which is arguably separate policy. "Preserve current CI behavior unless it is clearly duplicated policy" — the per-binding conditions are NOT clearly duplicated (they don't appear elsewhere). The variant list IS clearly duplicated (config files).

So the safest interpretation aligned with the instructions: make the **variant matrix** declarative and generated from manifests (the config files themselves, plus a runner mapping), and enforce that the workflow matrix stays in sync. This directly addresses "target support policy" and "duplicated policy" (config files ↔ matrix), and preserves all current per-binding behavior.

But "generate the checked-in workflow from manifests" suggests actually generating the workflow file, not just validating. So the generator should rewrite the matrix portion of ci.yml from the manifest.

I think I can proceed with a clear, defensible design without bothering the user, BUT the scope decision (matrix-only vs full job generation) is consequential enough and genuinely ambiguous that asking is warranted per the instructions. Let me ask a concise scope question. Actually, let me reconsider whether asking adds value or just delays.

The instruction says "You may use `ask_user` for consequential architecture or scope questions." It's permissive, not mandatory. I think a well-reasoned default is fine here, and asking could be seen as not decisive. However, the difference between scope 1 and scope 2 is significant in terms of how much of the workflow I generate and how much test surface exists.

Let me make a decision: I'll go with generating the entire `variants` job from a manifest that declares per-target runner and per-target job inclusion. This best satisfies "generate the checked-in workflow from manifests" and "declarative," and the per-binding conditions become testable declarative data. This is the more complete solution and the user explicitly said the policy "keeps drifting" — the binding conditions drift too.

Wait, but "Preserve current CI behavior unless it is clearly duplicated policy." If I move the binding conditions into a manifest, I preserve behavior exactly (same conditions, just relocated). That's fine — I'm not changing behavior, I'm relocating it. Good.

Hmm, but there's real risk in generating the *entire* workflow file vs. just the variants job. The hygiene/docs/required jobs are stable and not policy. I'll generate only the `variants` job's matrix and steps. The simplest robust approach: generate the whole `ci.yml`? No — that would require me to also encode hygiene/docs/required, which is not policy and shouldn't drift. Better: generate the matrix and the steps of the variants job.

Actually, generating partial YAML and splicing it into an existing file is fragile. A cleaner approach for a "generate the checked-in workflow" model:

Approach: Keep `ci.yml` as the source of truth for the non-variants parts, but generate the `variants` job content. The cleanest way to do this in practice is:
- Have a manifest (TOML).
- A generator script that produces the full `ci.yml` from a template (the static parts) + the manifest (the variants parts). The static parts are kept in the generator/template.

OR:
- A generator that produces ONLY the matrix and step list, and we validate that `ci.yml` contains exactly that. But splicing is fragile.

I think generating the entire `ci.yml` from a template + manifest is the cleanest and most "generate the checked-in workflow from manifests" aligned. The template holds the hygiene/docs/required jobs (static CI plumbing, not target policy). The manifest holds the targets and their job support policy. The generator combines them.

This means the generator's template reproduces the hygiene/docs/required jobs verbatim. That's fine — those are checked in as part of the generator.

Let me reconsider the per-step conditions and how to represent them declaratively. Each step has a `name`, a `run`, and an `if` condition expressed as a predicate over the variant. The predicates currently are:

| step | condition |
|------|-----------|
| configure | always |
| Build native library | ios- |
| Test native library | !ios- |
| Build Java FFM | !macos-arm64-egl && !ios- |
| Build Java JNI | !macos-arm64-egl && !ios- |
| Test .NET | !macos-arm64-egl && !ios- |
| Test Kotlin/Native | !macos-arm64-egl && !ios- && !windows- && !linux-arm64- |
| Test Rust | !macos-arm64-egl && !ios- |
| Test Swift | !macos-arm64-egl && macos- |
| Test Zig | !ios- |
| Build LWJGL | vulkan |
| Test Rust example | !macos-arm64-egl && !ios- |
| Build Swift example | macos-arm64-metal |
| Build Zig example | !macos-arm64-egl && !ios- |
| Run Zig readback | !ios- |

To represent these declaratively, I can give each variant a set of "capabilities" or "tags", and each step a condition over tags. Looking at the conditions, the relevant attributes are:
- os family: linux / macos / ios / windows
- arch: x64 / arm64
- render backend: vulkan / metal / opengl (egl)
- a special "binding-incapable" marker for `macos-arm64-egl`
- ios (cross-compile, build-only)

Actually, `macos-arm64-egl` is excluded from most bindings. Why? Let me think — it's an ANGLE-based EGL variant on macOS that probably can't host the JNI/native bindings well in CI. It's a special variant.

So the cleanest declarative model: each step declares which variants it runs on via a predicate, OR each variant declares a set of supported "suites". Given the irregularity, I think the most explicit and least error-prone is: **each variant explicitly lists the steps it runs**. But that's a lot of duplication and itself drift-prone.

Alternative: derive attributes from the variant name (and config), and express step conditions as predicates over attributes. The variant name encodes `<os>-<arch>-<backend>` (e.g., `linux-x64-vulkan`, `macos-arm64-metal`, `ios-arm64-metal`, `windows-x64-wgl`). The config file has `MLN_FFI_RENDER_BACKEND`. The `egl`/`wgl` suffixes are context providers.

Let me define step conditions in terms of derived tags:
- `build_only`: true for ios-* (cross-compile, no host execution)
- `host_bindings`: true for everything except ios-* and macos-arm64-egl (these can compile & run native bindings/examples)
- Actually macos-arm64-egl: host_bindings should be false.

Hmm, macos-arm64-egl breaks the clean os/arch/backend derivation. I think I should mark `macos-arm64-egl` explicitly as `host_bindings = false` (or a `bindings = false` capability) in the manifest, and keep it as an explicit override.

Let me define a clean capability model:
- Every target has: `variant`, `runner`.
- Derived automatically: `os` (linux/macos/ios/windows), `arch` (x64/arm64), `backend` (from MLN_FFI_RENDER_BACKEND).
- Capabilities declared per-target (defaulted by os):
  - `build` (build the native library) — always true
  - `test` (run native tests) — false for ios
  - `bindings` (run binding tests) — false for ios, false for macos-arm64-egl
  - `kotlin_native` — subset of bindings: false for windows, linux-arm64
  - `swift` — false except macos host
  - `lwjgl` — true for vulkan backend
  - `examples` — false for ios, false for macos-arm64-egl (like bindings)
  - `swift_example` — macos-arm64-metal only
  - `zig_readback` — false for ios

This is getting complex. Each step maps to a capability. The capability defaults can be computed from os/arch/backend, with explicit overrides per target.

I think rather than invent a capability DSL, the simplest, most explicit, and most testable declarative model is:

**Each target explicitly lists the set of CI "steps" it should run.** The manifest is a list of targets, each with `variant`, `runner`, and `steps` (a list of step names). The generator builds the job by, for each step name, emitting it (with `if` conditions that select exactly the targets that list it).

Wait, but emitting `if` conditions that select a specific set of targets requires generating `${{ matrix... }}` expressions. If a step runs on a subset, the cleanest matrix approach is to use `matrix.<step>` booleans: each matrix entry has e.g. `test_zig: true`, and the step's `if: ${{ matrix.test_zig }}`. That's very clean!

So the design:
- Manifest declares targets, each with variant + runner + a set of boolean step flags (or a list of step names → expanded to booleans).
- The generator produces a matrix where each entry has `mise_env`, `runner`, and one boolean per step flag.
- Each step in the job uses `if: ${{ matrix.<flag> }}`.

This makes conditions trivially correct and declarative. And `macos-arm64-egl`, `ios-*`, etc. just have different flag values. The current behavior maps cleanly:
- configure: always (no flag needed)
- Build native library (ios) / Test native library (else): two mutually exclusive steps. I can model as `native_test` flag: step "Test native library" if `matrix.native_test`, step "Build native library" if `!matrix.native_test`. Or simpler: a single `build_only` flag.

Let me map each step to a flag:
1. configure — always
2. Build native library — `if: ${{ matrix.build_only }}` (ios)
3. Test native library — `if: ${{ !matrix.build_only }}`
4. Build Java FFM — `if: ${{ matrix.java }}`
5. Build Java JNI — `if: ${{ matrix.java }}`
6. Test .NET — `if: ${{ matrix.dotnet }}`
7. Test Kotlin/Native — `if: ${{ matrix.kotlin_native }}`
8. Test Rust — `if: ${{ matrix.rust }}`
9. Test Swift — `if: ${{ matrix.swift }}`
10. Test Zig — `if: ${{ matrix.zig }}`
11. Build LWJGL — `if: ${{ matrix.lwjgl }}`
12. Test Rust example — `if: ${{ matrix.rust_example }}`
13. Build Swift example — `if: ${{ matrix.swift_example }}`
14. Build Zig example — `if: ${{ matrix.zig_example }}`
15. Run Zig readback — `if: ${{ matrix.zig_readback }}`

This is clean and fully declarative. The manifest lists, per target, which flags are true (or derives defaults). 

Now, should flags be explicit per-target or derived? Given "declarative" and "keep it from drifting," and that there are sensible defaults, I'll derive defaults from os/arch/backend but allow explicit per-target overrides. Actually, deriving introduces logic that could itself drift from intent. The most robust and reviewable is explicit declaration. But explicit for 11 targets × 14 flags is a big table.

Compromise: declare defaults per-os in the manifest, then per-target overrides. E.g.:

```toml
[defaults.linux]
runner = "ubuntu-latest"
# flags...
```

Hmm, but linux-arm64 needs a different runner (ubuntu-24.04-arm) and kotlin_native=false. macos-arm64-egl needs everything=false. ios needs build_only.

I think the cleanest is: each target declares its `variant`, `runner`, and a list of `steps` it runs (by step key). Steps are defined in a `[ci.steps]` table mapping key → {name, run, [default]}. Then per-target `steps` overrides. Actually let me make steps defined once (name + command), and each target lists which steps it includes. Flags are derived: `matrix.<step_key> = true` for steps the target includes. Steps not included → we still need the boolean false in matrix for the `if` to work. Actually with `if: ${{ matrix.<key> }}`, if the key isn't set it's falsy, but to be safe we set all flags to true/false explicitly per target.

Let me simplify the model:

```toml
# ci-targets.toml
[steps]
configure = { run = "mise run configure" }                # always
build_native = { run = "mise run build" }                  # build-only
test_native = { run = "mise run test" }                    # test
java_ffm = { name = "Build Java FFM binding", run = "mise run //bindings/java-ffm:build" }
...

[[targets]]
variant = "linux-x64-vulkan"
runner = "ubuntu-latest"
steps = ["test_native", "java_ffm", "java_jni", "dotnet", "kotlin_native", "rust", "zig", "lwjgl", "rust_example", "zig_example", "zig_readback"]

[[targets]]
variant = "ios-arm64-metal"
runner = "macos-latest"
steps = ["build_native"]
...
```

And `configure` is always emitted. `build_native` and `test_native` are mutually exclusive; the generator emits "Build native library" with `if: matrix.build_native` and "Test native library" with `if: matrix.test_native`.

Wait, but `configure` runs for both. And the build_native/test_native split is special. Let me handle it: each step gets a boolean matrix flag = whether the target includes that step. Steps with `if: ${{ matrix.<key> }}`. configure always runs.

This is clean. The matrix entry would be:
```yaml
- mise_env: linux-x64-vulkan
  runner: ubuntu-latest
  java_ffm: true
  java_jni: true
  ...
```

Hmm, that's verbose but explicit and correct. Alternatively, only set true flags and rely on absent=false. But actionlint/matrix may warn. Setting all explicitly is safer and more diffable.

Actually, a nicer approach: don't put booleans in the matrix. Instead, for each step, compute the set of variants that include it, and generate an `if` expression that lists them. But that's ugly for large sets. The boolean-per-flag approach is standard and clean.

OK here's my refined plan. But this is getting big. Let me reconsider scope once more against "Preserve current CI behavior unless it is clearly duplicated policy."

The current `Test native library` step runs `mise run test` (which depends on build). The `Build native library` step runs `mise run build` for ios. So for non-ios, it's `configure` then `test` (test depends on build). For ios, it's `configure` then `build`. Good — I'll preserve exactly.

I'm now fairly confident in the full-variants-job generation approach. But it's a substantial change. Let me ask the user a single concise scope question to confirm, since the instructions explicitly allow it and the scope is consequential. Actually — let me reconsider. Asking might be over-cautious. The user gave a clear directive: "Make it declarative, generate the checked-in workflow from manifests, and enforce that it stays in sync." That's a mandate to generate the workflow. The risk of asking is appearing indecisive; the risk of not asking is doing too much/too little.

I'll make a decision and proceed, but I'll ask ONE focused question only if truly consequential. I think the design I have is sound and aligns with the mandate. However, there's one genuinely consequential choice: whether to generate the **entire ci.yml** (including hygiene/docs/required) or just the **variants job**. Generating the entire file means the static jobs live in a template inside the generator; generating just variants means splicing.

```toml
variant = "linux-arm64-egl"
runner = "ubuntu-24.04-arm"
steps = ["test_native", "java_ffm", "java_jni", "dotnet", "rust", "zig", "rust_example", "zig_example", "zig_readback"]

... etc
```

Now mutual exclusivity: each target includes exactly one of build_native/test_native. I'll validate that.

Now the matrix entry per target: 
```yaml
          - mise_env: linux-arm64-egl
            runner: ubuntu-24.04-arm
            build_native: false
            test_native: true
            java_ffm: true
            ...
```

Hmm, that's a lot of booleans per entry (15 keys × 11 entries). It's verbose but explicit and stable. Alternatively, I can make the matrix leaner: only set flags that differ... no, explicit is better for correctness and the `if: matrix.<key>` to work reliably (absent = null, `if` on null is falsy, so absent works, but explicit false is clearer and matches typical matrix output). I'll set all explicitly for diff-stability and clarity.

Actually, GitHub matrix: a key absent in one include entry is fine; `matrix.<key>` evaluates to empty string/false. So I only NEED true entries. But for a clean, reviewable, stable diff, explicit booleans for all steps in all entries is best. I'll generate all explicitly.

Now the step `if`: `if: ${{ matrix.<key> }}`. For build_native/test_native mutual exclusion, each target has exactly one true, so exactly one runs. Good.

Now, the `configure` step stays static (always runs). The checkout + setup-ci-deps steps stay static. The job header, env (MISE_ENV), strategy (fail-fast, matrix header with include) — the include list is generated.

Let me now also reconsider: do I even need build_native vs test_native as two steps, or keep the original single-condition style? The original has "Build native library" (if ios) and "Test native library" (if !ios). My model reproduces this exactly with two steps gated on matrix.build_native / matrix.test_native. Good — behavior preserved.

Now, the generator. Language: Python (uv), type-checked with ty, linted with ruff (matches repo tooling, and the `.mise` dir is already type-checked by ty). The generator reads `.github/ci-targets.toml`, lists `.mise/config.*.toml` for validation, and rewrites the two sentinel regions in `.github/workflows/ci.yml`. 

Where to put the generator? A `scripts/` or a dedicated dir. The repo has `scripts/run-with-timeout.ps1`. There's also `.mise/` for mise-managed Python (ty checks `.mise`). Let me check if `.mise` has Python code. The hk.pkl references `ty check --error-on-warning .mise`. So `.mise` is expected to contain Python that's type-checked. Let me look at `.mise/bin` and `.mise/tasks`.

Let me check what's in .mise for Python and where uv project expects code. pyproject.toml has no packages. The `.mise` dir is type-checked by ty. So putting Python in `.mise/` makes sense if it's mise-support code. But a CI generator is more of a `scripts/` thing. However, to get ty type-checking for free (hk runs `ty check .mise`), I could put it under `.mise/`. Hmm, but that couples it. Let me check .mise/bin contents.

Let me also reconsider: should the generator be Python or something else? Python is available, has tomllib (3.11+, and they're on 3.14), good for tests (use unittest or pytest; repo uses uv + ruff + ty). I'll use Python with the stdlib `unittest` (or write tests that uv runs). Let me check if there's a test convention.

Let me look at .mise structure more and check for existing python tests.
The `ask_user` timed out. I'll proceed with a decisive design that satisfies all stated requirements. Let me gather the last details on where Python tooling lives and how it's tested.

metadata.preview:
name: Dev Container Image

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - .devcontainer/**
      - .mise/**
      - .miserc.toml
      - .github/workflows/devcontainer-image.yml
      - mise.lock
      - mise.toml
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - .devcontainer/**
      - .mise/**
      - .miserc.toml
      - .github/workflows/devcontainer-image.yml
      - mise.lock

display.text:
name: Dev Container Image

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - .devcontainer/**
      - .mise/**
      - .miserc.toml
      - .github/workflows/devcontainer-image.yml
      - mise.lock
      - mise.toml
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - .devcontainer/**
      - .mise/**
      - .miserc.toml
      - .github/workflows/devcontainer-image.yml
      - mise.lock
      - mise.toml
  workflow_dispatch:

concurrency:
  group: devcontainer-image-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' }}

env:
  IMAGE: ghcr.io/maplibre/maplibre-native-ffi/devcontainer

jobs:
  build:
    name: build / ${{ matrix.arch }}
    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request' || github.ref != 'refs/heads/main'
    permissions:
      contents: read
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.runner }}
    timeout-minutes: 90
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          - arch: amd64
            runner: ubuntu-latest
          - arch: arm64
            runner: ubuntu-24.04-arm
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-ci-deps
      - name: Install image tooling
        run: sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install --no-install-recommends --yes skopeo
      - name: Build image
        run: mise run //.devcontainer:build --tag "maplibre-native-ffi-devcontainer:ci-${{ matrix.arch }}"

  release:
    name: release / ${{ matrix.arch }}
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name != 'pull_request'
    permissions:
      contents: read
      packages: write
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.runner }}
    timeout-minutes: 90
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          - arch: amd64
            runner: ubuntu-latest
          - arch: arm64
            runner: ubuntu-24.04-arm
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@df4cb1c069e1874edd31b4311f1884172cec0e10 # v6.0.3
      - uses: ./.github/actions/setup-ci-deps
      - name: Install image tooling
        run: sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install --no-install-recommends --yes skopeo
      - name: Log in to GHCR
        run: echo "${{ github.token }}" | docker login ghcr.io --username "${{ github.actor }}" --password-stdin
      - name: Build and push image
        run: mise run //.devcontainer:build --tag "$IMAGE:main-${{ matrix.arch }}" --push

  manifest:
    name: manifest
    permissions:
      packages: write
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name != 'pull_request'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: release
    timeout-minutes: 10
    steps:
      - name: Log in to GHCR
        run: echo "${{ github.token }}" | docker login ghcr.io --username "${{ github.actor }}" --password-stdin
      - name: Publish multi-arch tag
        run: |
          docker buildx imagetools create \
            --tag "$IMAGE:main" \
            "$IMAGE:main-amd64" \
            "$IMAGE:main-arm64"

Now let me check whether mise is available in this environment to run things, and what tools exist. Let me verify the environment can run mise/uv/python so I can actually test the generator and run lints.

Let me check the environment.
Let me verify the toolchain is available so I can build, test, and lint.

part.text:
mise and actionlint aren't installed globally, but uv and python3 are. The repo uses mise to manage tools. Since mise isn't available here, I'll need to install actionlint via some means to validate the workflow, or just rely on careful YAML. Let me try to install actionlint quickly to validate the generated workflow.

Actually, the AGENTS.md says to use `mise exec -- ...` for commands not in tasks, and to run lints. But mise isn't installed. Let me check if I can install it. Actually, the environment may not have mise. Let me check if there's a way. The task is to write code + tests + lint. Let me see what I can run.

Let me check if I can install actionlint via uv tool or go or download a binary. Actually, let me check internet access. I'll try to get actionlint to validate the final workflow YAML. But first, let me focus on writing the generator and tests, and validate YAML parses with Python's yaml.

Is PyYAML available? Probably not by default. I can use uv to run with a dependency. Let me check.

Let me first plan the whole thing and write it, then figure out validation.

Let me decide the final structure:

Files to create:
1. `.github/ci-targets.toml` — the manifest (source of truth).
2. `scripts/ci_targets/__init__.py` — package
3. `scripts/ci_targets/__main__.py` — CLI entry (generate/check)
4. `scripts/ci_targets/generator.py` — core logic
5. `scripts/ci_targets/config.py` — maybe combined
6. `scripts/ci_targets_test.py` or `tests/` — tests

Hmm, the repo has no `scripts/` Python structure. Let me keep it simple but idiomatic. Where does the repo expect Python? pyproject.toml is at root. Let me make a small package. Given ty checks `.mise`, and there's no existing python package layout, I'll create `scripts/ci_matrix/` as a self-contained module with a test file, and wire `uv run` to execute it.

Actually, let me reconsider placement. Options:
- `scripts/ci_matrix.py` (single file) + `scripts/test_ci_matrix.py` — simplest. uv can run it. Tests via `uv run python scripts/test_ci_matrix.py` or pytest.
- A package under `tools/`.

The repo uses `scripts/run-with-timeout.ps1`, so `scripts/` is an accepted location for scripts. But `.py` there gets ruff/ty via hk (glob `**/*.py` excluding third_party). Good — ruff and ty will cover it. Wait, ty only checks `.mise` per hk.pkl: `check = "uv run ty check --error-on-warning .mise"`. So ty won't check scripts/. I should extend ty to also check the generator, or move it under a checked location. Let me make ty check both `.mise` and `scripts`. Actually I'll update hk.pkl ty step to include the generator path. Hmm, but `ty check` takes paths. I'll set it to `ty check --error-on-warning .mise scripts` — wait does ty accept multiple paths? ty is astral's type checker; `ty check <paths>`. Should accept multiple.

Let me keep the generator in `scripts/` and update ty to check it too. Or, simpler and cleaner: put the generator under `.github/` next to the manifest? But hk ruff glob is `**/*.py` so it'll be covered regardless; ty needs explicit path. I'll update the ty check to include the generator.

Decision: put generator at `scripts/ci_matrix.py` (single module) + `scripts/test_ci_matrix.py`. Update hk.pkl ty to `uv run ty check --error-on-warning .mise scripts/ci_matrix.py` (or just `scripts`). Actually to be safe and match intent (type-check repo python), I'll set `ty check --error-on-warning .mise scripts`.

Wait, but ty might find other issues. Let me keep it scoped: `ty check --error-on-warning .mise scripts/ci_matrix.py scripts/test_ci_matrix.py`. Hmm that's verbose. Let me just do `scripts`.

Now the generator design (final):

`scripts/ci_matrix.py`:
- Constants for paths: MANIFEST = `.github/ci-targets.toml`, WORKFLOW = `.github/workflows/ci.yml`, CONFIG_DIR = `.mise`, repo root detection.
- `load_manifest()` → parses TOML, returns structured data (steps list, targets list). Validate: step keys unique; target variants unique; each target's steps reference valid step keys; each target has exactly one of build_native/test_native (I'll make these two required mutually-exclusive steps).

Actually, let me reconsider build_native/test_native mutual exclusivity enforcement. The native step: in original, non-ios runs `mise run test` (which builds+tests), ios runs `mise run build`. So every target does exactly one. I'll enforce exactly one native step per target. Let me define a concept: a "native" step group {build_native, test_native}; each target must include exactly one.

Hmm, to keep it generic, maybe simpler: just treat them as normal steps and assert every target includes exactly one of {build_native, test_native}. I'll hardcode that pair as the "native steps" and validate. That's fine.

- `validate_config_files(targets)`: for each target.variant, assert `.mise/config.<variant>.toml` exists. Also assert no config file lacks a target (every config.*.toml has a target) — this is the core sync enforcement. This prevents drift both ways.
- `build_matrix(targets, steps)`: produce list of matrix entries (dicts): mise_env=variant, runner, and <step_key>: bool for every step. Bool true iff step in target.steps.
- `render_matrix_block(matrix, steps)`: produce the YAML text for the include entries (indented), with markers.
- `render_steps_block(steps)`: produce the YAML text for each step: `- name: <name>\n        if: ${{ matrix.<key> }}\n        run: <run>` (with optional comment line above). Indented to match.
- `regenerate(workflow_text, ...)`: replace content between markers `# BEGIN GENERATED ...` / `# END GENERATED ...` for both regions.
- `check(text)`: regenerate and compare; return whether identical.
- CLI: `generate` (rewrite in place), `check` (exit nonzero if drift), `print-matrix` (debug).

Markers in ci.yml:
```
          matrix:
            include:
              # BEGIN GENERATED MATRIX — managed by `mise run ci:generate` (.github/ci-targets.toml)
              <entries>
              # END GENERATED MATRIX
        steps:
      ... static steps: checkout, setup, configure ...
          # BEGIN GENERATED STEPS — managed by `mise run ci:generate` (.github/ci-targets.toml)
          <steps>
          # END GENERATED STEPS
```

Wait, indentation: the variants job's steps are at 6 spaces? Let me check ci.yml indentation. Jobs at 2 spaces (`  variants:`), keys at 4 (`    strategy:`), matrix at 6, include at 8, entries at 10 (`          - mise_env:`...), entry fields at 12. Steps: `    steps:` at 4, step items `      - uses:` at 6, step fields at 8. Let me re-read.

From ci.yml:
```
  variants:           # 2
    name: ...         # 4
    runs-on: ...      # 4
    strategy:         # 4
      fail-fast:      # 6
      matrix:         # 6
        include:      # 8
          - mise_env: # 10
            runner:   # 12
    steps:            # 4
      - uses: ...     # 6
      - run: ...      # 6
      - name: ...     # 6
        if: ...       # 8
        run: ...      # 8
```

So:
- matrix entries: list items at 10 spaces (`          - `), fields at 12 spaces (`            runner: ...`).
- steps: list items at 6 spaces (`      - `), `if`/`run` at 8 spaces (`        run: ...`).

I'll generate with correct indentation.

Now the configure step. In original:
```
      - run: mise run configure
```
configure is always-on, static. Keep it static (above the GENERATED STEPS region). Actually, I could make configure part of generated steps too, but it's always-on and simple. I'll keep it static for clarity, and the GENERATED STEPS region starts after configure.

Wait, but then the "Build native library"/"Test native library" steps are generated (they're build_native/test_native). And configure is static before them. Order: checkout, setup, configure (static), then generated steps (build/test native + bindings + examples). Good.

Let me also preserve the kotlin comment via the step's `comment` field. Multi-line comment: I'll emit each line as `      # ...` above the step. For kotlin_native, comment lines:
```
      # linux-arm64: no Kotlin/Native host — https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/KT-36871
      # windows: no MSVC target — https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/SKIKO-446
```

Good.

Now, the matrix entry rendering. For each target:
```
          - mise_env: <variant>
            runner: <runner>
            build_native: <bool>
            test_native: <bool>
            ... all step keys as booleans ...
```
I'll render booleans as `true`/`false`.

Now let me think about determinism/ordering: targets in manifest order (preserve); steps in manifest order for both matrix columns and steps block. Good.

Now tests (`scripts/test_ci_matrix.py`):
- Test `build_matrix` produces correct booleans for a couple of targets.
- Test mutual exclusivity validation rejects a target with both/none native steps.
- Test unknown step key rejected.
- Test config-file consistency: target without config file rejected; config file without target rejected.
- Test `render_matrix_block` and `render_steps_block` produce expected snippets (golden small).
- Test `regenerate` is idempotent (regenerate twice == once) on a sample workflow.
- Test `check` detects drift.
- Maybe an end-to-end: generate, then check passes.

I'll use stdlib unittest. Run via `uv run python scripts/test_ci_matrix.py` or `uv run python -m unittest`. Actually I should add a mise task `ci:generate` and `ci:check` and a test task. Let me see how to wire.

mise tasks are in mise.toml ([tasks.x]). I'll add:
```toml
[tasks."ci:generate"]
description = "Regenerate the CI variants workflow from .github/ci-targets.toml."
run = "uv run --no-project python scripts/ci_matrix.py generate"

[tasks."ci:check"]
description = "Fail if the CI variants workflow is out of sync with its manifest."
run = "uv run --no-project python scripts/ci_matrix.py check"

[tasks."ci:test"]
description = "Run the CI generator unit tests."
run = "uv run --no-project python -m unittest discover -s scripts -p 'test_ci_matrix.py'"
```

Wait, `uv run --no-project python` — does that work without a project? uv run python uses the project env (root pyproject requires python>=3.14). Since python 3.14.4 is installed and matches, `uv run python` should work. But uv run might try to sync the project. Let me use `uv run python` (it'll use the .venv). Actually the repo has `.venv` already. `uv run python` will use it. Let me not add `--no-project`. Hmm but uv run resolves the project deps (dev group). Should be fine.

Actually, for scripts that need only stdlib, I want them to run reliably. `uv run python scripts/ci_matrix.py` should work since stdlib only. Let me verify after writing.

For the CI hygiene enforcement: wire `ci:check` into `ci:lint` task so CI fails on drift. The `ci:lint` task currently runs hk fix + git diff checks. I'll add `ci:check` to it. Actually, `ci:lint` runs `hk fix --all` (which may regenerate? no). Let me add the sync check as its own step in `ci:lint`:

```toml
[tasks."ci:lint"]
run = [
  "dprint output-resolved-config > /dev/null",
  "hk fix --all",
  "uv run python scripts/ci_matrix.py check",
  "git update-index -q --refresh",
  "git diff --exit-code -- . ':(exclude)mise.lock'",
  "git diff --cached --exit-code -- . ':(exclude)mise.lock'",
```
